item.title: The funny fridge
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this. Could probably fit more as a -J, but +1 nonetheless

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
GAH!

Who are you and how did you get into my Loneliness Bunker!? D:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Three possibilities:
- The ritual summons an eldritch horror, and binds it to the Task of disrupting its own summoning ritual. (This seems to be the author's intent, based on comments.)
- The ritual summons an eldritch horror, but said eldritch horror is having none of it and keeps hitting the snooze button because screw this nonsense dimension.
- The ritual summons an incredibly inept eldritch horror, who keeps tripping over its own portal and accidentally noping out of the ritual.

I prefer the last one because I think it's funnier.

item.title: Re: Unspoken take
item.content:encoded: I'm telling you guys. S&C Plastics makes this soooooo good. I can see it now. A buncha bored teenagers starting a cult after the sheriff already told them twice not to loiter in front of the gas station unless they're gonna buy something. The eldritch abomination that can't quite seem to cross over into our realm without tripping over its own portal. The shoddy worksmanship. The sheer stubbornness of retrying the ritual because technically we can't fill out the form saying what Classification to give it yet, and there was no direct oversight until a Director started questioning where all this funding was going. It's genius, I tells ya.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
WarpZone,

You do realize you're answering a whole bunch of comments from 2013, don't you? It's not like this is an active conversation anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Fridual." :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Write it up, man. Worst case scenario, it ends up with a permanent home on a Sandbox Page. There are worse fates.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My friend, just read up on the S&C Plastics Canon, and I believe all will become crystal-clear.

(BTW, any relation to the late, great Antisocialfatman?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
XD Fuck!

Why did that make me laugh so hard!?

"This is fine." XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I mean, they won't know for sure how dangerous it is until they can keep it summoned long enough to see what it does. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My headcanon is that the ritual actually successfully summons a horrific cosmic entity, but said entity is a huge doofus and keeps accidentally botching the whole thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They had lots of (conflicting) advice from existing cult members and a magic circle that lights up when you get a step right. It's not a question of if. It's a question of when. :)

If it makes you feel any better, just pretend their final instructions say to pour ice-cold coffee on the ground in step 37, when actually tepid water would have sufficed, and they will never know it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting. It made me think more of that cosmic horror on The Tick who struggled to maintain a lease in an Earth apartment until the stars would be right for his return.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoever wrote this, I love you. Marry me… *trips over power cord, unplugging the fridge and leading to the cessation of anomalous activity and the demanifestation of the entity*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Making sure, but was this the Scp that was a really long ritual that had to do with a fridge, but the being inside noped all attempts by just reaching out an arm and knocking over the fridge? If it is not, then maybe the two rituals are connected?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For all of you who tried to google translate it, je ne suis pas mort means "I am not dead." The article had just enough ridiculousness to be amusing while staying interesting.
+1

item.title: Re: Unspoken take
item.content:encoded:
With the final memorandum removed, this isn't a terrible idea. With one visible step being "figure out the correct previous step (sic)," it is completely plausible that the Foundation simply discovered one working ritual.

The scip itself isn't terrible either, being reminiscent of the pop culture warnings to avoid similar rituals because they "might just work." That some in this group of 100 figured out a seemingly random and markedly strange series of tasks to convert a normal refrigerator into a summoning portal for alien beings is precisely the sort of schtick Foundationverse bureaucrats would groan about having to handle.

item.title: Unspoken take
item.content:encoded: I just wanted to share a different head cannon which seems more valid in my head. A ritual was figured out, but it wasn't the intended ritual. I propose that the refrigerator, with the correct set of steps, could be used to summon all sorts of different things. This just happened to be the first set of valid inputs to get a result, and they stopped experimenting with new possible steps each time they got a 'confirmation'. So for all we know it could also summon a mate for SCP-682…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Brilliant. The most inept of all eldritch abominations. This tickles all the right spots in my brain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1731 was discovered in the desert outside of █████, NV on 01/01/2007 after the Foundation heard reports of a group conducting a "fridge ritual" in the area.

You had me at "Fridge Ritual"

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. It does have shades of lolfoundation if not a joke entry (though I don't have anything against lolfoundation… as long as it's done with restraint) but it's still just believable enough to make you not call bullshit on it being a proper SCP.

It actually kind of feels like the exact kind of stuff the SCP Foundation was created to investigate: something that looks normal at first but does just the most random freaky shit under the right conditions.

In short, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mostly like this. The sillyness is fine and I really like the core self-terminating aspect, but what bothers me is that the Foundation kept testing too long. After they found out what the ritual entailed and did - namely summon a horror from beyond- they should have stopped. It is great that the nature of the ritual makes successful freeing of the entity nigh-impossible, but repeating at that point seems like nothing less than an attempt to free it!

Perhaps the ritual has a memetic aspect compelling those fully aware of the ritual to repeat it? That would reinforce the repetitive aspect and give a damn good reason to only list fragmentary directions here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
has it been done? Its been a long time and I'd like that idea to become reality.
If it has not been done, I'd do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…

I…

I'm not sure what exactly happened here…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hmmm….I have an idea, but it might be too similar to the "missing number".
It's a period between February and March. The original Gregorian calendar had it, splitting the year into 13 months with an equal number of days. Clearly, 13 does not divide 365 evenly, but Septembuary don't care. The year/days changed to match the calendar, resulting in some pretty catastrophic side effects from the uneven stop-start motion of the Earth.

The 12 month calendar was a proto-Foundation containment measure. It isn't a rational calendar, the months are all different lengths for starters, and people kept independently re-inventing Septembuary. So the PF inserted a crafted meme, code named "Leap Day", that causes people to disregard the fact that the calendar doesn't actually fit the year. It was a rush job, they were on the verge of a somethingK-scenario, so the memetically induced "blind-spot" only lasts about 5 years unless the subject is re-exposed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dare you to write an SCP (joke or not joke, either way) called "Septembuary".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I like here is that it is applying a bit of real world logic to the unreality and narrative causality of scp-verse.

There are all sorts of things irl that self-negate, pretty much all the transuranic elements tear themselves apart microseconds after coming into existence, for example. Species that excrete chemicals poisonous to their own metabolism, frequently to the point of localized extinction. The Easter Island natives, who decided that giant square foreheads were more important than food, shelter, and the means to evacuate their self-made death trap. At least one scp should represent this sort of "useless machine" dynamic.

You know what makes it even more bizarre? Not realizing the log dates are in european format. The first death happened in Septembuary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I DON'T CARE IF THE STARS ARE RIGHT
I HAVE A HANGOVER

*squamish snooze button slam*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kic[UNREADABLE] while pun[UNREADABLE]ctus.

So I'm supposed to kick something while punching a cactus?

Part of the problem with [UNREADABLE] is that it doesn't properly display the amount of spacing that would allow the reader to infer various possible solutions; as such, maybe this is really Kick a man named Stan while punching a shark tied to a cactus.
+1 anyway.

"Lickin a beagle, breedin seagulls, you can be regal if you're pissin on eagles!"
"Punchin a whale and shippin' a sail and sailin a ship while ticklin' sailors,"
"Nailin' a snake!" "Swearing in braille!" "Wearin' a cake!" "Growin' a tail!"
"Pissing off your boss is not illegal!"
"But pissing ON your boss, now that's illegal."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the basic premise (a paranormal phenomena that cock-blocks itself), but I found the randomness of the details off-putting. There are other ways of getting across that the set-up process is really, really complicated besides turning the article into a mad-lib. (no vote)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's clearly a bit too silly to really deserve an upvote

I hate this mindset so much.

And it use to be much, much sillier, trust me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are you implying that silly things are bad scips?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but I'm not sure why. It's clearly a bit too silly to really deserve an upvote, but it's still so plausible. If you take the SCP-verse as true, then this strikes me as something which could totally exist in that verse. It's just so not like an SCP, which is brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm torn. I find this to be well written and all, but it just doesn't really grab me. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't understand the problem people are having with this. It seems fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love that not only did they finish researching it, but they finished researching it because "Well, we know what it does now; nothing more to see here" instead of "Holy fuck, this was way more dangerous than we were expecting".

The SCP itself is also delightfully absurd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. By performing a shitload of crazy, specific steps, you can make this thing do something weird, but not all that exciting, that always results in it messing up what you did. I have to wonder if that last bit is actually intentional; people keep waking up this thing that thought it had sealed itself up with a series of 'locks' that no one could ever figure out, and then they did, and now it just wants to go back to sleep.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…:U

I can't please everyone. Hm.

/goes to contemplate

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I took it out for now, because I guess it was kinda obvious and I wasn't too attached to it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is borderline, but… definitely crosses that border. Well done.

I like that the Foundation were able to actually figure out the ritual. Even though there was no real point.

I also like the addendum, though it is a bit on-the-nose. Maybe tweak - but keep.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was pretty much how I felt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could not agree with this more. Upvote here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I showed it to other people and they didn't seem to get the idea behind it without it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last addendum makes my upvoting finger go limp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great, just great. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I what.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: These are the people who made a moon base by throwing countless people into a teleporting kiddy pool one at a time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Point taken, footnotes removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unless that was meant to be the original documents before the Foundation figured out the rest

Yep.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am just wondering why it says that all the steps are known, but there are UNREADABLE parts… I get you might want some mystery, but it seems redaction or expungment might be better. Unless that was meant to be the original documents before the Foundation figured out the rest. +1 anyway, I was just curious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would upvote if only because you had a perfectly valid opportunity to use AWCY and yet you controlled yourself. Not bad. It's Holders-y in a good way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP is patently absurd, but I have to admit that I found myself smiling a bit by the time I got to the end.

The footnotes are bothering me though; neither of those are necessary, just write the four or so extra words into the sentences themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where does it meed editing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not too sure what I think about this. It stretches my suspension of disbelief quite a bit, but it's one of those very stretches that stopped me from downvoting.

So, neutral vote.

EDIT: Changed since you removed the last addendum, which was my biggest problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is actually pretty funny and has a certain vibe of AWCY. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could use quite a bit of editing. Not sure how I feel about the concept, either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, this again. Nope.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still don't buy that the Foundation could have figured out all of the missing/incomplete steps to this ritual through experimentation alone. The steps that they started with were complicated and highly specific. I doubt the missing ones were any simpler. Nope, I don't think they could get that with all the experimentation/time in the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This might be my all time favorite safe class.
I also LOVE the fact that all you really have to do to contain it is leave it there. And even if it breaches containment, it does pretty close to nothing.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like I said in chat, Azzle. This doesn't catch my eye, or my attention.
